steps.title: Identify what the phrase “for one” modifies
steps.content: The sentence lists two pivotal events: the founding of the League of United Latin American Citizens (in a specific year) and the Katzenbach v. Morgan court decision in 1966. The phrase “for one” (meaning “for example”) marks the first event as one example in the series, so it must attach directly to the year of founding—not float free or attach to the second event.

steps.title: Test each choice against punctuation conventions
steps.content:
A transitional phrase marking one item in a series must be set off by a comma before it, and a period after it creates the sentence boundary that introduces the next example as a new sentence.
  • Choice A — 1929. For one, the: The period comes after 1929, so “For one,” appears to introduce the second event (the Katzenbach decision) as “one example,” which reverses the intended meaning. ✗
  • Choice B — 1929, for one, the: Uses only commas, producing a comma splice—two independent clauses joined by a comma alone. ✗
  • Choice C — for one, the: Omits the year entirely, removing the historical fact, and also creates a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice D — 1929, for one. The: A comma after 1929 sets off “for one,” attaching it to the founding; the period then closes that sentence and a new sentence begins with “The” to introduce the Katzenbach decision. ✓
D is correct.
